title: BERT & Embeddings Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of contextual embeddings, BERT fine-tuning, and semantic search.

questions.question: What makes BERT's embeddings 'contextual', unlike Word2Vec embeddings?

options.text: BERT uses larger vocabulary sizes so it can handle more words
BERT attends to all surrounding words when computing each token's vector, so the same word gets different vectors in different contexts
BERT is bidirectional, meaning it reads text from right to left
BERT was trained on a larger dataset than Word2Vec

questions.question: What learning rate should you use when fine-tuning BERT and why?

options.text: A high rate like 0.1 to quickly adapt the large model to your task
A standard rate like 0.001 as commonly used for training from scratch
A very small rate like 2e-5 to avoid catastrophic forgetting of the pretrained knowledge
Learning rate doesn't matter for fine-tuning pretrained models

questions.question: Why is the `sentence-transformers` library recommended over raw BERT for semantic similarity tasks?

options.text: sentence-transformers models are always larger and more accurate than BERT
Raw BERT's [CLS] token is a poor sentence representation; sentence-transformers are specifically trained to produce meaningful sentence-level embeddings via mean pooling and contrastive learning
sentence-transformers can handle images as well as text
It is impossible to use BERT without the sentence-transformers library

questions.question: In a semantic search system, why are document embeddings computed once and cached, rather than recomputed on every query?

options.text: The embedding model can forget the documents if they are not recomputed frequently
Document text changes frequently so embeddings must stay fresh
Embedding computation is expensive; since static documents don't change, caching avoids redundant computation and dramatically reduces search latency
Cached embeddings are more accurate than freshly computed ones

questions.question: Which BERT variant is the best choice when inference speed is the top priority?

options.text: bert-base-uncased — the original is always optimal
roberta-large — larger models are always faster due to better hardware optimization
distilbert-base-uncased — 60% smaller, 2x faster, while retaining 97% of BERT's accuracy
multilingual-e5-large — it supports more languages and thus handles queries faster
